data.title: UniCredit Q2: Record Quarter, Dividend To Watch, Caution Advised
data.description:
Banks represent one of the most astonishing narrative turnarounds we have seen in this century. After the Great Financial Crisis, they have usually traded at depressed valuations and were despised and shunned as if they were spreading mortal illnesses. The reputational damage suffered by the whole industry was substantial. Then Covid came, and the post-pandemic rebound brought along red-hot inflation. Central banks hiked interest rates, and banks thrived, being one of the best-performing sectors in the market.

UniCredit (OTCPK:UNCRY, OTCPK:UNCFF) distinguished itself even more positively from the crowd. Back in 2020, it traded as low as EUR 7 per share. You can now buy a share for EUR 60. Since my last article written in April 2024, the stock has returned a whopping 125%, 100 percentage points above the S&P500. Now, this is alpha, and it comes from one of the largest European banks, not from some unknown stock.

The big question is whether UniCredit still offers upside or not. Well, the bank just released its 1H earnings, and we have some food for thought. Let's digest it together.

Last year, I pointed out that UniCredit's success was mainly linked to a successful turnaround, partly linked to Italy's growing household gross wealth that needs to be managed, generating commission fees. On the other hand, UniCredit focused on reducing costs and generating more value under the leadership of its CEO, Mr. Orcel. Since he took the reins in 2021, UniCredit has delivered outstanding results, and it has become one of the key players in the so-called bank-consolidation-risk that is going on in Italy and in Europe.

Surely, high interest rates helped many banks and their net interest revenue. But now that the ECB is lowering rates as inflation seems tamed and the economy needs some help to combat the drawback caused by geopolitical tensions (rare earth control, gas supplies, trade tariffs, wars, etc.), we need to understand how UniCredit expects to keep posting great results.

Just a quick recap before we start. Banks are a different type of business from industrial or service companies. In a way, their product is capital itself. Moreover, they are highly leveraged and regulated. This requires us to use appropriate metrics to analyze their results.

First, we have to consider their ROE and ROA as the most important profitability metrics, together with net interest margin and the ever-so-important cost-to-income ratio. Banks are simply obsessed with this ratio to prove to investors that their operational efficiency is improving. The target is to be below 50%. Secondly, we need to assess credit quality and risk metrics. Non-performing loans represent the credit risk exposure, and we want them to be as low as possible. Sometimes, it is important to know the coverage ratio to see the cushion a bank has against credit losses. Usually, conservative banks are above 100%. Regulators require banks to have capital buffers. So, capital adequacy is measured with the CET1 ratio, which we expect to be at least 10%. Finally, while the PE ratio is useful, banks are better assessed through the price-to-book ratio, which compares the share price to the net value of their assets.

Well, UniCredit reported its 1H ever, with 2Q25 beating net profit and RoTE expectations, leading the bank to up its 2025 guidance and its 2027 ambition.

Core revenue was EUR 5.9B, +1.3% YoY. Net profit of EUR 3.3B (ex one-offs EUR 2.9B) was a record, and RoTE improved to 24.1%% for the quarter. Net interest income decreased only by 0.3% Q/Q and by 2.8% YoY despite lower interest rates. Probably, UniCredit's customers are as happy as investors about these results, because it means UniCredit slowed the pass-through process. The cost-income ratio was below 37.8%, which makes UniCredit a leader to this extent. The CET1 ratio was 16.2%.

Now, overall, the results were good, thanks to UniCredit's balanced mix of revenue streams, which is made up of NII as well as fees, loans, and deposits. However, analysts and investors were waiting for the earnings call to talk with UniCredit's CEO about the withdrawal from the Banco BPM bid, its doubled stake in Greece's Alpha Bank, and its increased stake in Commerzbank (OTCPK:CRZBF) to 28%. When asked about these topics, Mr. Orcel replied in this way:

This quarter is full of noise. Our withdrawal from BPM, the restructuring of our hedges on Commerzbank, the internationalization of life insurance, the one-off risk and charges anticipated in the future, rates coming down, I would say, faster than we anticipated they would come down at the beginning of the year, volatility from Liberation Day, and everything else. But very clearly, the improvement -- the underlying results for Q1 and Q2 are that underlying, and they are stronger than last year as a whole, and in most of the KPI lines.

So, the difficult task of this earnings report was to filter through the noise and focus on the fundamentals. Looking at the numbers we have seen above, we can consider UniCredit's first part of its turnaround completed, with top-notch metrics reached across the board. The second part of the program will focus more on new opportunities and on completing the operational changes still needed.

As a result, when we look at the upgraded guidance and the 2027 ambition, we see a few things that are noteworthy. First of all, UniCredit's distribution remains outstanding. Even though the stock has been a 9x since its Covid-lows, its dividend still yields 5%, with the perspective of another strong increase since UniCredit announced that it will pay in November the interim 2025 dividend of EUR 2.1B (+46% YoY). Secondly, the buyback program was upgraded to EUR 3.6B and will be executed starting this month. So we are talking about a highly rewarding stock that keeps returning billions into the shareholders' pockets while delivering outstanding capital returns.

However, when we look at the 2027 ambition, we can't help but notice that UniCredit expects to keep growing in the next two years, but the pace seems to be slower. Therefore, I think what will support the stock will be the bank's ability to keep returning cash to the shareholders.

Right now, UniCredit trades at a TTM P/B of 1.6 with its fwd P/B estimated to be around 1.3, which helps us understand that investors are expecting more growth from UniCredit. So, looking ahead, I believe the stock's upside doesn't rest on skyrocketing revaluations but more on strategic optionality. Investors seeking the right balance of income and value exposure to Europe's banks should consider UniCredit, as it deserves a place at least on the watchlist. Investors looking for a bank moving at the same accelerated pace we have seen over the past few years should, on the other hand, be a bit more cautious because it seems UniCredit's growth pace will gradually slow down.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Arthur J. Gallagher: A High-Quality Stock That's Worth The Premium
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome William Davis as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Introduction

Arthur J. Gallagher & Co. (NYSE:AJG) is a promising investment opportunity. Despite the stock being at a premium, its consistent double-digit growth, strong profitability, and margin expansion suggest that the stock still has room to grow. Furthermore, AJG’s strategic acquisitions enable the company to continue its rapid growth by pursuing external growth opportunities along with its organic growth.

The Company Overview

Insurance Brokerage

While Arthur J. Gallagher & Co. has multiple revenue sources, the heart of the business is its robust insurance brokerage. During the first quarter of 2025, AJG’s brokerage operations generated $3.31 billion in revenue. This was a sizable leap from the first quarter of 2024, where the company generated $2.86 billion. While this rate of growth is impressive, it's even more impressive given that 9.5% of the growth was organic. This highlights the firm's ability to grow its client base and existing relationships without solely relying on acquisitions. The profits from the insurance brokerage have risen dramatically. It far surpassed the $652.6 million earnings from the first quarter of 2024 by gaining $816.1 million in the first quarter of 2025, further entrenching it as the primary engine of the company.

Risk Management

The primary risk management subsidiary of Arthur J. Gallagher & Co. is Gallagher Bassett. In addition to delivering stable performance, this section of the business is increasingly playing a strategic role in the company’s core operations. Over the first part of 2025, it posted revenue of $373.4 million, which represents a 5.8% year-over-year increase. While it is relatively modest compared to the brokerage side of the business, it represents a stable source of income that adds to margins. Also, risk management demand increases during market downturns as businesses seek to protect themselves. This provides the business with revenue and profits that could buoy it through recessions or other periods of uncertainty.

HR Consulting

The third major market for Arthur J. Gallagher & Co. is its HR consulting operations. This portion of the business seeks to assist businesses and business owners with managing human resources and benefits planning. This includes regulatory compliance issues as well as long-term planning for the enterprise. This wing of the company helps to increase its attractiveness to potential clients by allowing the firm to provide a holistic package of services. As a result, this allows the company to build very deep relationships with its clients. Furthermore, it creates opportunities for cross-selling products, which increases the revenue per client.

Growth Strategy

Arthur J. Gallagher & Co. pursues a two-pronged growth strategy. It concentrates on organic growth through internal expansion and cross-selling of its products. However, it also pursues a highly aggressive acquisition model. This approach enables the company to enjoy a far more rapid rate of growth. In the first quarter of 2025 alone, the company acquired 11 companies. Together, these firms contribute almost $100 million in revenue. To further expand AJG’s insurance operations, it acquired Woodruff Sawyer. This was a large deal that immediately added $250 million in recurring revenue.

AJG's Catalysts And Strengths

Arthur J. Gallagher’s growth is bolstered by durable strengths stemming from its acquisition strategy and adoption of AI. Among the most important is its ability to continue pursuing an aggressive acquisition strategy. In the first quarter of 2025, the company went through 11 mergers, which added $100 million in annualized revenue. Furthermore, the company’s acquisition of Woodruff Sawyer has added another $250 million in revenue. This rapid growth comes after 20 consecutive quarters of double-digit growth, which further reinforces the company’s operational efficiency and strategic discipline.

The company’s effective adoption and aggressive plans for AI deployment are another strength. Insurance and risk management companies must assess voluminous amounts of data in order to arrive at profitable premiums and other fees. The use of AI has the potential to streamline this process, which may dramatically increase operational efficiency. As a result, AJG may be positioned to underwrite insurance for lesser served markets or those that are particularly high risk, which has the potential to greatly increase revenue and margins.

Arthur J. Gallagher & Co. has provided double-digit year-over-year growth. From the second quarter of 2023 to the first quarter of 2025, the company’s quarterly revenue has risen from $2.44 billion to $3.08 billion. Much of this growth can be attributed to the company’s aggressive but strategic approach to acquiring new businesses.

Not surprisingly, the company has also enjoyed a surge in net earnings. In the second quarter of 2023, the company recorded net earnings of $235.8 million, which grew to $486.6 million in the first quarter of 2023 before ballooning to $704.4 million in the first quarter of 2025. If you are looking for a company that consistently boosts both its sales and profits, AJG certainly makes a strong argument for itself. Additionally, investors who are worried about its strategy of aggressive acquisitions may be soothed to see that so far the plan is working.

In addition to being focused on growth, Arthur J. Gallagher & Co. is also efficient at leveraging its newly acquired businesses into profitable components. The company has rapidly increased its net earnings margin. From the end of 2024 through the first quarter of 2025, the firm went from a net earnings margin of 13.5% to 23%. During the same period, AJG increased its operating margin from 19.78% to 21.24%. While this growth would be impressive for most companies, it is particularly eye-catching for a company with so much acquisition activity.

The company’s cash and equivalents decreased from $1.55 billion in the first quarter of 2023 to $1.42 billion in 2024. Not surprisingly, this decrease is largely the result of Arthur J. Gallagher & Co.'s acquisitions requiring it to use some of its cash. Additionally, the company has increased its total debt from $6.70 billion to $8.08 billion. Reflecting the company’s acquisition approach, the total assets have swollen from $45.1 billion to $63.0 billion.

While the company’s cash has decreased and its debt has increased, it has been successful in boosting cash flow. From the first quarter of 2023 to the second quarter of 2024, cash flow increased from 428.5 million to $908.8 million. Despite the spending spree, the cash flow is growing faster than the acquisition expenses.

Arthur J. Gallagher & Co. is currently trading at a P/E of 46.61, which is far more than the P/E of its peers. For example, Marsh McLennan (MMC) has a P/E of 25.66, Aon (AON) is at 29.81, and Brown & Brown (BRO) has a value of 29.43. In addition to being far higher than its peers, AJG’s current P/E is also above both its three- and five-year averages. For example, the five-year P/E is 38.84 while the three-year is at 44.08.

In terms of P/S ratio, AJG is also higher than the insurance industry average. Its 6.81 P/S ratio is almost double the industry average. Additionally, its EV/EBITDA ratio is also substantially higher than the industry average. Its ratio of 21.5 far surpasses the industry average of 9.2. This implies that the market places a high premium on Arthur J. Gallagher & Co.’s ability to consistently generate cash flow. While these factors suggest that the stock is overpriced, its much faster rate of growth and higher profitability suggest that it may still have room to rise.

Estimating Arthur J. Gallagher's Valuation

A simplified discounted cash flow analysis can help with assessing the intrinsic value of Arthur J. Gallagher & Co. By using 2024 free cash flow of $2.441 billion as the base and projecting a five-year phase with declining growth rates, it may be possible to gain a more detailed understanding of the value of the company. After this period, a terminal growth rate of 2.5% can be applied. This rate is consistent with mature, cash-generative companies in the insurance sector.

These results suggest that the enterprise value is $77.94 billion. After considering AJG’s $16.7 billion in cash and $13.1 billion in total debt, the equity value came to approximately $81.54 billion. Dividing this by the total shares outstanding yields an intrinsic value of $318.39 per share. Therefore, it appears that AJG is between fairly priced to slightly undervalued.

From a valuation standpoint, upside appears limited above $340 per share, where AJG would trade at more than 25 times forward free cash flow. This is substantially above historical norms. Conversely, a decline towards $285 would offer a more compelling target. However, margin erosion or acquisition integration issues would signal potential weaknesses emerging in the company’s growth strategy, which may indicate a need to exit the position.

Assessing The Risks

The insurance brokerage market is highly competitive and undergoing substantial consolidation. While AJG’s acquisition strategy has driven much of its growth, it also exposes the company to fiercer competition for acquisition targets. More specifically, Marsh McLennan, Aon, and private equity firms aggressively bid on many of the same acquisition opportunities. This creates a risk of driving up the acquisition multiples, which dramatically increases the risk of overpaying for targets. Additionally, the increasingly competitive environment results in rising costs related to marketing, client acquisition, and post-merger employee retention. These costs all represent meaningful pressure on margins.

Acquisitions are central to AJG’s strategy, but it has its own operational and financial risks. Successful integration requires harmonizing company cultures, integrating IT systems, and retaining key personnel under compressed timelines. Failure in any one of these areas can reduce the expected ROI from a deal and drain management focus. Due to this threat, it is imperative for AJG to avoid this by continuing to be strategic in the acquisitions it makes.

Arthur J. Gallagher & Co. is a prime target for cyberattacks due to the large amounts of client data that it holds. Despite insurance companies investing heavily in cyber defenses, the frequency of threats continues to increase across the industry. In 2020, AJG suffered a breach that led to a $21 million class-action settlement affecting over 3 million individuals. Beyond the direct financial consequences of these breaches, they also damage client trust and brand image. Unfortunately, AJG’s acquisition-driven growth model adds complexity to this challenge. Newly acquired firms may bring security vulnerabilities that are difficult to identify and integrate.

Conclusion

Arthur J. Gallagher stands out in the insurance industry. It is among the largest players while also offering a unique combination of attributes for businesses in this space. In particular, it offers a chance to invest in a company that exhibits sustained revenue growth, increasing profit margins, and the defensive characteristics of insurance companies.

The key to AJG’s explosive growth has been its aggressive but thoughtful approach to acquisitions, which have added $350 million in revenue in just a single quarter. Despite its aggressive expansions, the company has continued to keep its margin healthy and minimize friction. While this may be something to keep an eye on, there is currently little evidence that the growth is causing problems for the company.

Due to Arthur J. Gallagher & Co.’s overall health and aggressive growth strategy, the stock should be considered a “Buy” for those looking for a long-term investment. It can also be a viable option for those wanting a stock that can offer stability if an economic downturn were to occur.

data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: Pernod Ricard: Fairly Valued Brand Powerhouse For Dividend Income
data.description:
Pernod Ricard (OTC:PRNDY) is one of the giants in the spirits industry. It names itself as a number one international premium spirits maker with 94 production sites in 24 countries. Its products are distributed in 160 countries. In terms of volume, its best selling brands are Absolut, Jameson and Ballantine’s.

Similarly to other spirit stocks, Pernod Ricard’s shares have more than from the highs of over €200 per native share. Pernod Ricard is maneuvering in the challenging operating environment and protecting its financial position with decisive actions. The shares have fallen to a fair valuation given conservative assumptions. Even though the outlook is unclear, a long-term income investor could take advantage of purchasing shares in an entrenched company with centuries of staying power.

Well-diversified house of brands

Pernod Ricard’s history dates back to 1805 with the establishment of Pernod. By the early merger with Ricard and as a result of a series of acquisitions over the years, the company consists of over 200 brands, including some of the most popular alcohol drinks and balanced across the spectrum of different types of spirits. Its annual revenue is approximately €11-12 billion. The members of the founding Ricard family still have a significant ownership (15%) in the company and Alexandre Ricard is the acting CEO.

Pernod Ricard follows an irregular financial year. In February, Pernod Ricard reported its H1 2025 results. The organic revenue declined 4% and EPS decreased 11%. However, profit from recurring operations declined only by 2%. The company signals the year to be transitory. Fortunately, free cash flow developed well to €440 million, growing 46%. The negative development was rather even in different regions.

In April, Pernod Ricard reported third-quarter results. The organic revenue declined 3%. In H1 it had a negative €110 million impact on its profit from recurring operations and now expects a similar impact in the latter part of the year. The revenue has suffered the most in Europe and global travel retail. In China the Q3 decline was much less than year to date (-5% vs. -22%).

Maneuvering the stormy waters

Spirit companies are maneuvering against several external headwinds. There’s a question of decreasing alcohol consumption in the developed markets partly offset by the trend of premiumization. The industry has gone through phases of overstocking and destocking clouding the financials with swinging revenue. Decreasing purchasing power has led consumers to decrease consumption and choose more affordable brands. Finally, tariffs by China and the U.S. are potentially weakening the position of European brands in the most important alcohol markets.

Alcohol appears to be one of the levers that politicians are pulling in the ongoing trade war. Currently, Pernod Ricard is expecting an annual cost of €200 million, because of the tariffs in China and the U.S. Interpreting results in the coming quarters will likely be complex, if the U.S. customers have been buying a lot before the tariffs and exchange rates distort the figures. The impact of U.S. tariffs are likely to impact the company later in the near future.

China recently imposed a 34.9% levy on European brandy, which was at least an intermediate resolution to an unclear situation. Companies that commit to minimum pricing agreements could be exempt from the duty, this is what Pernod will do. Overall in China, sales of cognac fell 29%, but premium cognac fared better with sales falling 8% last year. The sales of Martell have declined sharply, but brands such as Absolut, Olmeca and Jameson are growing.

Fortunately, Pernod Ricard is widely diversified geographically. India has become the second biggest market for Pernod Ricard before China (12% vs. 9% of revenue) by growing fast. While sales in China declined by 25%, revenue in India grew 6% in H1. India is the second largest volume market for spirits and the largest market for whiskey, which is the number one category for Pernod Ricard.

Pernod Ricard has done several significant actions to mitigate the headwinds. One of them is portfolio optimization. In 2024, it sold a group of wine operations and ten brands for an undisclosed sum. Wines represented around 4% of its revenue and the company retained some of its premium wines and champagnes in its portfolio. The same year it also sold a couple of local brands in Finland and it closed divestments of Clan Campbell and Becherovka brands. And now it is about to sell its Indian whisky brand, Imperial Blue, for €413 million in order to focus on more profitable and fast growing premium brands.

According to Reuters, the company has recently launched a restructuring program in which it is about to organise its brands into two units aiming to cut costs by one billion euros by 2029. The same goes for other spirit producers. Pernod Ricard believes it is gaining market share in the premium segment in most of its operations around the world.

In order to counter the potential long-term adverse trends, Pernod Ricard is active in the ready-to-drink category (RTD). It is a way to get to the hearts and minds of younger generations, which are often considered to consume less alcohol than the ones before. In H1, the RTD category grew 15%. Like other alcohol producers, Pernod Ricard is also exploring opportunities in the THC-infused drinks. It is rumoured to be interested in Brez

Pernod Ricard’s net debt to EBITDA ratio stands rather high at 3.5x. It has a debt load of €12 billion to take care of. The debt has steadily risen from €7.4 billion in FY2021. Most of its debt is fixed-rate and nominated in euros. Approximately half of its debt is due after more than five years. Many of its bonds maturing in the coming years are issued at low interest rates (1-2%), meaning interest expenses are likely to increase in the near future.

Not expensive but not screamingly cheap either

The company updated its outlook for the year in April. It expects low single-digit organic sales decline but steady operating margin. It pursues to protect the cash flow. Analysts are estimating revenue to fall 5.3% and earnings per share to decline 11%. As the revenue, costs, tariffs and foreign exchange rates are all in motion, it is only possible to make a best guess and preferably a range of outcomes, where the earnings are going. The company also reports adjusted numbers and analyst estimates reflect those.

Pernod Ricard is currently trading at a forward P/E-multiple of 14x and EV/EBITDA of 11.7x. Historically, the shares have traded at P/E-multiple of 20-21x and EV/EBITDA of 15x. Pernod Ricard’s earnings per share have grown at 4% CAGR for the past decade, which would not justify historical valuation level. Neither does its average return on capital.

When estimating a fair value for the shares, let’s use FY 2026 analyst EPS estimate as a starting point to mark it as an earnings through. Then we estimate them to recover at a historical rate, apply a terminal multiple of 18x and a discount rate of 10%. This would give us a fair value of €94, slightly below today’s share price of €97. Using a 4% growth rate for the full 10 years or a terminal multiple of 20x, would give us a fair value of €100.

In relation to its competitors, Pernod Ricard (RI) is trading at approximately similar valuation depending on the metric used. Its historical performance is not far off from the rest either. There’s at least one conclusion to be drawn from a peer comparison. A prudent investor looking for a decent safety margin, rather applies a historically lower terminal multiple as done in the fair value calculation above.

Pernod Ricard is followed by 20 analysts with an average target price of €108.

Dividend yield is attractive

Pernod Ricard pays a dividend twice per year. Last year the total dividend was €4.7. The company has announced the first dividend payment of €2.35 with an ex-date 23rd of July. If the second instalment, usually paid in November, follows the pattern of two previous years, the total dividend will be of a similar size than last year. The payout would result in a dividend yield of 4.8%.

From the perspective of the free cash flow, the dividend payment appears stretched. The dividend payout totals €1.2 billion annually. Pernod Ricard’s free cash flow has been approximately €1.3 billion on average. Last year the free cash flow declined 33% to €963 million. However, the capital expenditures and investments in inventory were record high in FY2023 and FY2024, the company expects lower spending and tight cost control to boost cash flow.

Conclusion

Pernod Ricard will likely come out stronger from the challenging operational environment in the spirits industry. Its powerful brands and geographical and product diversification are the company’s backbone. Pernod Ricard has been and is taking decisive actions to prune its portfolio and protect its profitability. The outlook is unclear, to say the least, but the valuation has reached a fair level and shareholder returns are supported by a juicy dividend.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Life360: 3 Key Developments To Watch In Q2 Earnings
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I initiated my coverage on Life360, Inc. (NASDAQ:LIF) last month:

  • Life360: This Is What A Best-In-Class Consumer Subscription Looks Like

In that prior analysis, I shared a bullish sentiment towards the stock and recommended a ‘Buy’ rating for long-term investors. In particular, I highlighted their consistent revenue growth, recent transition to profitability, and best-in-class revenue quality for a consumer subscription business.

In this analysis, I specifically want to preview the company’s upcoming Q2 earnings release, coming on August 12th prior to market open. In particular, I believe there are three key developments that investors should monitor in the earnings release: user activity, profitability profile progress, and advertising platform performance.

User Activity

User activity will always be the leading temperature gauge for the health of Life360’s business. When users are frequenting the app, they’re most often happy, engaged customers. But when user activity takes a nosedive, it typically signals a customer that will be on the way out the door at some point in the near future. It’s this very dynamic that makes user activity a critical metric for investors to watch.

In Q1, Life360 posted ~83.7 million monthly active users across the globe. Although this number pales in comparison relative to the company’s long-term goal of 150+ million monthly active users, Life360 is operating at a vast scale nonetheless.

Year-over-year growth in global monthly active users came in at 26% in Q1. At the same time, Q1 revenues grew by 32.5% year-over-year. So not only is Life360 demonstrating their ability to consistently grow their customer base, but they’re proving that they can generate more revenue from each customer than they have historically—both of which are telltale signs of a healthy, growing business.

Interestingly, Life360 also reported that their iOS app is among the highest daily active users across all apps in the US. In terms of daily active users, the Life360 Family Locator app ranks among a slew of recognizable names such as Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN), Pinterest (NYSE:PINS), Roblox (NYSE:RBLX), and Netflix (NASDAQ:NFLX). This high usage is a testament to the stickiness of Life360’s products.

Looking ahead to Q2, I’d like to see many of the same trends that we’ve been seeing recently from Life360. Of course, growing monthly active users is a baseline expectation for investors. But more importantly, I’d like to continue seeing Life360’s monetization outpace their raw user growth. Doing so could be taken as a leading indicator of the company’s ability to continually add new revenue streams and enhance user monetization.

Profitability Profile Progress

Life360 has made significant strides in terms of their profitability over the last few quarters. They’ve generated a net profit in each of the last three quarters. Moreover, over the last four quarters, they’ve generated a cumulative net profit of $9.6 million.

Although a net profit of ~$10 million is fairly inconsequential for a business generating close to $400 million in annual revenue, it’s a particularly important milestone for a company that sells monthly subscriptions to consumers. Although the recurring, predictable nature of these consumer subscription businesses is a major benefit in the eyes of investors, it also comes with some drawbacks. Namely, these companies often have to front large, upfront investments in sales and marketing efforts in order to acquire new customers. It’s often this very reality that causes software companies to run heavy, net and operating losses for a sustained period of time.

But by crossing over into operating and net profitability while continuing to grow their customer base, Life360 is showing that their customer acquisition efforts are working in an efficient manner. Now, they’re able to pursue both growth and profitability at the same time.

In their upcoming Q2 earnings release, I’m going to be watching both gross and operating margins for the company. In Q1, Life360 posted a staggering gross margin of 80.6%. This was up over 650 basis points from their gross margin in Q4 2024, and almost 400 basis points higher than their Q1 2024 margin.

It will be telling to see if Life360 is able to sustain gross margins above 80%. If so, it’s a positive signal to their competitive advantage and pricing power in the market.

As one might expect, the company’s margins have been a bit more uneven from quarter to quarter on the operating side. Their Q1 2025 operating margin was 2.1%. This was down from their Q4 2024 margin of 5.1%, but up from their Q1 2024 margin of -8.15%. Over the last four quarters, the company is essentially operating at a breakeven pace, with an operating margin of 0.2%.

In Q2, I’ll be watching for operating margins to once again land in the black. As I see it, anything in the 2%-5% range like they’ve done in the last couple of quarters would be a positive sign. With their strong top-line growth, I don’t see it as completely necessary for the company to throttle things back at the expense of growth. They’re still very much a growth-first company, and should operate as such. Profitability at this stage is more of an indication of efficient spend instead of a necessity.

Advertising Platform Performance

Perhaps the most interesting development stemming from this Q2 earnings call will be the discussion around Life360’s improved advertising platform. The company announced in June that they were enhancing their ad platform with two new features: Place Ads and Uplift.

At its core, Place Ads allows advertisers to target Life360 users with offers based on their location behavior. For example, one of the early advertising partners for the platform was with Uber (NYSE:UBER). When the Life360 services detected that users had landed at the airport, they were then notified of offers from Uber to purchase a ride from the airport. The campaign was very successful, leading to over 100,000 rides booked on the Uber platform.

On the flip side, Life360’s Uplift ads enable advertisers to improve attribution, as they can now track when a user visits a particular location (i.e., a store) after being shown an advertisement. Performance marketing has long struggled with attribution in a cookie-less physical world. But with offerings such as Life360’s Uplift, such attribution is becoming readily available to advertisers.

Among their three revenue streams—subscription, hardware, and other—Life360 classifies revenue from their advertising platform as ‘Other’. In Q1, the company posted $12.8 million of Other revenue. However, this revenue came before the formal launch of their enhanced ad platform.

Looking ahead to their Q2 earnings release, it will be particularly interesting to listen to the commentary from management on the earnings call regarding their expectations for the enhanced ad platform. As of Q1, their guidance calls for between $55 million to $65 million of Other revenue in full year 2025. If they were to hit the midpoint of their guidance by end of year, it would imply that Life360 would need to book just over $47 million of Other revenue in the final three quarters of the year.

However, with the formal rollout of the enhanced ad platform not actually taking place until the Q2 books have already been closed, I’d expect that most of the revenue for the Other stream will be back-loaded into the latter half of the year. In Q2, I’m expecting modest growth in the Other revenue stream to around $13 million to $14 million, but for it to accelerate sharply from there in Q3 and Q4.

Valuation

Life360 continues to command a premium valuation, especially for a business whose end users are consumers. However, as I noted in my last analysis, Life360’s economics are not what you may expect from a consumer subscription business. Namely, they have robust revenue retention at ~100%. And with year-over-year revenue growth exceeding 26% and consensus revenue estimates pegging 2025 revenue growth to remain steady at that level, it becomes much easier to see why Life360’s valuation is as rich as it is.

Since my last analysis, Life360’s valuation multiple has expanded considerably. At the time, just over a month ago, the company was trading for an EV/Revenue multiple of 11.6x, and a forward multiple of 9.8x. Today, those multiples have expanded to 14.1x and 11.9x, respectively.

I’ll be the first to admit that these multiples initially sound pricey. But as a first-pass rule of thumb, I often look for software companies with strong retention to be trading for an EV/Revenue multiple that is close to half their revenue growth rate.

In Life360’s case, their 26% growth rate makes their 14.1x EV/Revenue multiple appear just a touch expensive. But with such strong underlying fundamentals driving, I remain confident in the company’s future over the long-run. However, should their upcoming Q2 earnings hint at any developing cracks in the foundation of their business, I’d be ready to dial up the scrutiny towards their valuation. Barring that outcome, I maintain the same sentiment as I shared in my last analysis:

In the end, although Life360’s valuation may be rich at the moment, I believe their underlying fundamentals back it up. For investors with a longer time horizon that are less sensitive to entry prices, I believe that now could be a reasonable entry point for long positions.

Risks

Data privacy and regulatory scrutiny are key risks worth highlighting for Life360’s stock. Considering that they have real-time location data—in addition to a host of other data points—on over 80 million people worldwide, data privacy is of the utmost importance for Life360.

For this very reason, I’m interested to see how users respond to the rollout of the new ad platform features. While I believe in the merit of the capabilities from a business standpoint, it remains to be seen if there will be any form of pushback from users as advertisers look to profit from Life360’s valuable data. Of course, advertisers would argue that users should be pleased with the updates, as it will allow them to receive better, more-applicable offers.

Regardless, investors should be aware that Life360 carries a fair amount of privacy risk. Any sort of data breach or compromised security could have a tangible impact on the company’s stock.

Conclusion

I maintain the sentiment that Life360 has built a robust business. They’ve grown their active user base almost fourfold since 2019, and have demonstrated their ability to monetize each individual user at higher and higher rates over the years. In addition, they are trending towards consistent profitability, yet are continuing to grow at a high rate.

In anticipation of their Q2 earnings release, investors should pay close attention to updates concerning Life360’s user activity, profitability profile progress, and advertising platform performance. As I see it, these three factors will be leading indicators for what’s to come for Life360 stock. In the meantime, I am maintaining my ‘Buy’ rating for long-term investors for Life360 stock.

data.title: DFAT: A Low-Volatility, Attractively Valued Small-Cap ETF
data.description:
The Dimensional US Targeted Value ETF (NYSEARCA:DFAT) is among the small-cap funds focused on the value investment theme. But DFAT is not only a value fund. It offers a combination of value and quality in the smaller capitalization category that has been done well over time. With a historical outperformance and contained volatility, DFAT seems well-positioned to benefit from a more favorable setup for small caps over the coming quarters, given easing interest rate expectations, while stretched valuations in the overall market suggest further upside for small caps.

ETF Description & Highlights

Using Dimensional's factor-based methodology, DFAT is an actively managed ETF that seeks long-term capital growth, without tracking any index in particular. To achieve this, its core investment thesis relies on holding a portfolio of small- and mid-cap stocks with attractive valuations and healthy fundamentals.

As of July 18, 2025, DFAT managed $10.9 billion in assets invested in a portfolio of nearly 1,500 holdings, with an average market cap of $3.45 billion. The allocation is heavily skewed towards micro and small caps, which account for 38.4% and 55.6% of the fund, respectively, while mid caps make up only 4.4%. Based on this profile, we are going to use as the benchmark for DFAT the Russell 2000 index, which is represented below by the iShares Russell 2000 ETF. As we can see below, this index has a similar allocation structure, but with a lower average market cap of $2.54 billion compared to DFAT's $3.45 billion.

DFAT's largest holding is IT logistics player TD Synnex (0.68%). Other top positions are from financial services, with Ally Financial (0.64%), First Horizon (0.62%), Webster Financial (0.53%), SouthState (0.53%), and Franklin Resources (0.52%). The fund's top ten holdings are complemented by a diversified group of companies: The Mosaic Company (0.58%) from basic materials, Toll Brothers (0.57%) from consumer discretionary, industrials WESCO International (0.55%), and energy player Ovintiv (0.52%).

The following table includes six value-oriented ETFs focused on smaller companies that will serve as the peer group for DFAT in this analysis. The first is the Vanguard Small-Cap Value ETF (VBR), the largest in the group, with $55 billion in AUM, tracking the CRSP US Small Cap Value Index. The next fund is the Avantis US Small Cap Value ETF (AVUV), which employs an active, factor-driven approach, targeting stocks with low valuations and strong profitability. The Schwab Fundamental U.S. Small Company ETF (FNDA) uses a fundamentally weighted methodology, favoring profitable and low valuation multiples names, The iShares S&P Small-Cap 600 Value ETF (IJS) offers exposure to the small-cap value stocks through a rules-based index, while the Dimensional US Small Cap Value ETF (DFSV) applies a similar factor methodology used by DFAT but with just nearly 1,000 holdings. Finally, the Invesco S&P SmallCap 600 Revenue ETF (RWJ) is based on the S&P SmallCap 600 index, but with a revenue-weighted structure that results in a greater exposure to smaller capitalization companies.

Source: Morningstar, consolidated by the author

DFAT's largest exposure is to financial services, which accounts for 30.3% of the fund's total assets. That's followed by industrials at 14.9%, and consumer staples at 14.2%. The fund still has a significant allocation to technology at 8.3%, energy at 8.7%, consumer discretionary at 6.9%, healthcare at 6.4%, and basic materials at 5.6%, while there are only small positions in communication services at 3.4%, real estate at 1.1%, and utilities at a modest 0.2%.

Relative to the Russell 2000 index, DFAT is overweight in financial services (+12.3%), consumer staples (+4.2%), and energy (+4.0%), three typical value sectors. On the flip side, it is underweight in healthcare (−8.7%), technology (−7.8%), and real estate (−5.8%) as a result of above-average valuation multiples seen in these areas.

Breaking down the fund's sector allocation, the heavy allocation to financial services is largely driven by an overweight stance in banks, which alone represent more than half of the sector, while it still has significant positions in the insurance and consumer finance industries.

The industrials sector remains the second-largest DFAT sector, despite its slightly underweight allocation. While machinery is the largest segment, holdings are distributed across areas such as trading companies, trucking, building products, and construction & engineering, providing a well-diversified exposure across the sector. By contrast, in consumer staples, the allocation is more concentrated in the food segments, with positions in food producers, distributors, and retailers.

Outside of these three largest sectors, DFAT has a fairly diversified allocation across the overall market. Specialty and auto parts retailers are the leading segments in consumer discretionary. Meanwhile, attractive valuations are a key factor for the fund's overweight stance to this sector, while its limited exposure to technology is driven by modest holdings in software and semiconductors, as the fund favors low valuations of electronic and communication equipment providers.

DFAT's large stance in financial services is not the norm among value-oriented peers. With peers' typical allocation below 21% to the sector, only DFSV has a similar 30% allocation. On the other hand, funds like VBR, FNDA, and IJS have a special emphasis on real estate, likely influenced by the sector's high profitability, which contrasts with DFAT's marginal 1.1% position in the sector.

That said, DFAT has a strong sector correlation of over 0.95 with AVUV and DFSV. However, it is interesting to see funds like FNDA and particularly RWJ employing different allocation strategies. This is evidenced by their relatively weaker sector correlations under 0.70 relative to DFAT, hinting that these funds may become an interesting combination for investors looking to diversify their portfolios within the small-cap value space.

Attractive Valuation

DFAT's P/E of 11.5x represents a meaningful 24.6% discount to the Russell 2000's 15.2x. This gap reflects two key factors: The first one is the fund's large position in financial services, a value sector by nature, along with an underweight exposure to higher P/E multiples seen in technology, as the sector's common P/E multiples are over 20x, even in the small caps category. The second factor comes from DFAT's stock selection, which is reflected in consistent valuation gaps of at least 20% across most sectors, including financial services and technology.

There is no secret that small caps have been facing a challenging environment over the past few years, as higher rates have been a drag on the companies' bottom line. This scenario seems exacerbated in the value space, with DFAT showing negative historical earnings growth, led by financial services.

But if we look at profitability measures, the situation is different. DFAT's EBITDA margin of 19.6% is 5.9 percentage points higher than the benchmark, while its ROE of 7.1% is better than Russell's 0.9%. Key contributions to this higher EBITDA come from higher margins in most sectors, particularly healthcare and energy, while DFAT's advantage in ROE is largely due to healthcare and technology. Overall, this paints a mixed figure for DFAT, as the fund shows more attractive valuation and profitability measures, which is consistent with the fund's mandate, but lags on growth, given its more defensive profile.

DFAT's valuation is among the lowest in the peer group, below funds like VBR (P/E 13.3x) and FNDA (P/E 14.7x), particularly the latter as it carries a higher P/E profile across most sectors. But overall, DFAT's valuation remains in line with peers AVUV (11.6x), RWJ (11.7x), and DFSV (11.1), as those share a limited stake in pricier areas like technology, with an overweight allocation to value sectors such as financial services, energy, and consumer staples.

Outperforming The Benchmark With Low Volatility

DFAT has outperformed the Russell 2000 index over time, with annualized returns of 11.7% (3-year) and 17.3% (5-year) that have been over 3% higher than the benchmark, despite a relatively weaker 2024, when it lagged by 3.6%. Compared to peers, DFAT has also done better than all funds over the past three years, while its 5-year performance has been mixed, as AVUV and RWJ were the top performers in the category.

On the volatility side, DFAT has seen more contained price moves than the Russell 2000, as its beta, ranging from 1.06 to 1.18, is lower than the benchmark. Meanwhile, its downside capture ratio has also been below the benchmark, evidencing a historical tendency to show narrower losses during downturns. DFAT has also been a less volatile instrument than all peers, which have seen high beta and steeper drawdowns over the past year. The exception is VBR, which has delivered more controlled volatility. Still, all funds have shown measures way higher than the S&P 500, the reference for volatility metrics, as small caps have historically been an area more vulnerable in risk-averse environments.

With a solid average daily volume ($16.04 million) and a tight spread of 0.05%, DFAT offers reasonable tradability measures, with bid-ask spreads in line with the most liquid peers like DFSV and FNDA, despite their heavier trading volume. The fund's modest turnover of 9.0% is also at the lower end of the group, while its expense ratio of 0.28% is above the average, likely driven by its actively managed style. It offers a 1.52% TTM dividend yield, slightly lower than the peer group average, but still above the Russell 2000's 1.11%, driven by the fund's large exposure to financial services. In addition, the fund's tracking error of 13% is somewhat expected for an active fund, while its Sharpe ratio in the range of 0.39 and 0.70 positions DFAT among those with the best risk-adjusted returns, alongside AVUV and RWJ, as a result of the combination of its overall outperformance and relatively low volatility.

Final Thoughts

Based on DFAT's stock selection approach and sector allocation structure, I view DFAT as a deeply value-oriented fund. Meanwhile, its profitability measures also stand out compared to a small-cap broader index like the Russell 2000.

The fund's low valuation and quality tilt matter a lot in this current environment. While positive earnings releases so far and expectations for trade deals prior to Trump's administration deadline to raise tariffs have raised stocks to fresh records over the past few days, mixed macroeconomic data and still elevated interest rates are expected to temper the risk appetite for smaller capitalization companies.

In this scenario, despite current headwinds, small-cap valuations are becoming more appealing now. With that in mind, DFAT seems a good fit for investors looking to position in advance of a more dovish stance from the Fed as we move toward the year-end. After all, DFAT's valuation offers upside, and its historical low volatility is expected to provide a more balanced risk/reward when compared to the Russell 2000 and most small-caps peers.

data.title: W.R. Berkley Q2 Review: Strong Results Are Priced In
data.description:
Shares of W.R. Berkley (NYSE:WRB) have been a strong performer over the past year, adding 31%. However, the stock has lost about 10% from its high, suggesting good news from strong operating results and a strategic investment could now be in the price. I last covered shares of WRB in March, arguing that the stock was fully reflecting its strong operating performance and that shares would likely trade flattish. This has come to pass with the stock down about 3%, though given the market added 15% during this time, a sell rating rather than a hold would have been justified. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit WRB.

In the company’s second quarter, W.R. Berkley earned $1.05, which beat estimates by $0.03. WRB earned $3.1 billion of premiums, up $250 million from last year with written premiums reaching a record of $3.4 billion. Underwriting income was $261 million, resulting in a current year combined ratio of 91.6%. The company had $99 million of catastrophe losses, which is a relatively normal level for the second quarter of the year. Seasonally, late Q3-early Q4 can have the most cat losses given the typical timing of US hurricane season.

Looking at segment results, insurance generated pre-tax income of $513 million from $491 million as net premiums rose by about 10%. Its combined ratio improved 30 bps to 92.1%. Reinsurance and monoline activity was up just over 2% to $369 million. Profits rose by $3 million to $127 million as its combined ratio ticked up to 87.4% from 81.8%.

Within insurance, it is primarily focused on growing liability and short-tail lines while more slowly growing auto and workers’ compensation. While workers’ comp has been a solid category, strong profits have reduced pricing here and weighed on returns. This has caused WRB to step back. I continue to view management’s willingness to pull back from areas with weakening returns as a key positive differentiator.

Indeed, WRB is targeting 8-12% growth this year, down from its historic target of closer to 10-15%. This reflects a more competitive pricing environment with management showing discipline in slowing growth rather than writing policies, which could prove to be uneconomic. Management described the operating environment as “interesting.” Similar to Chubb (CB), it noted that there is weaker pricing in large accounts, causing the company to shift resources.

Still, despite these pressures, WRB generated an average rate increase of 7.6%. Given increased pressure on national accounts, I do expect premium inflation to slow. As you can see below, premium inflation is slowing nationally, and this deceleration to 3.3% is a reason why shares have pulled back from highs.

A strong two years of premium inflation has helped WRB and others in the industry restore underwriting margins. With margins now strong, it appears some competition for policies is increasing. Still, premiums are at least keeping pace with overall inflation. As such, I do not see direct pressure on underwriting margins over the next year; rather, I see WRB’s combined ratio stabilizing. I would note that while management sees tariffs as manageable, elevated pharmaceutical tariffs could be a headwind. I view any tariff pressure as likely to be transitory and eventually result in higher premiums that recapture lost income.

Elsewhere, WRB generated $379 million of net investment income, and the company remains a primary beneficiary of elevated interest rates. It has a 5.25% new money yield. For comparison, its portfolio has a book yield of 4.7%, so we should continue to see the investment portfolio’s yield rise at least through year end in my view. This will support growing investment income, towards $400 million by early 2026.

Importantly, the company’s $31.2 billion investment portfolio is high quality. It has $26 billion of fixed income and cash in the portfolio. Its fixed income portfolio more than fully covers its $21.5 billion in insurance reserves. Beyond this, WRB has a return-seeking allocation, including $2.7 billion of alternatives and a $1 billion trading account. I view its portfolio as adequately conservative and do not expect material credit losses.

This conservatism is consistent with how WRB manages its business. It has a strong balance sheet with 23.4% debt to capital. This positions the company to continue returning capital to investors. While they have not done material buybacks, management sees that as an ongoing tool. Its share count is only down about 0.8% over the past year. In June, the company paid a special dividend of $0.50, on top of its regular $0.08 dividend. Special dividends have been the primary focus of capital returns. To an extent, this likely speaks to management’s view about the attractiveness of the stock. Additionally, the Berkley family likely prefers special dividends, given they provide a source of income unlike share repurchases.

Frankly, WRB’s capital return policy is very conservative, and management prefers retaining capital to grow premiums, given it is generating a ~20% return on equity. I do not expect this preference to change, though if premium growth decelerates further that could lead to more capital for buybacks and special dividends. Additionally, as discussed previously, Mitsui is in the process of acquiring a 15% stake in the company, though it has not yet hit the 5% threshold. It aims to complete purchases by March of next year. These share purchases in the open market are likely to continue to have a supporting technical effect on shares.

Back in March, I saw W.R. Berkley earning $4.45-$4.60. I see results as being on track to this outlook, and so long as the hurricane season is not worse than expected, I see WRB earning at least $4.45 on a normalized basis. With fundamentals unchanged, I still see valuation as full. Shares have a $24 tangible book value. Given its ~20% ROE, I would normally justify a 2.5x price to book multiple, for an 8% underlying return.

That points to fair value of about $60. On top of this, WRB has about ~$5 of excess capital beyond what it needs to run its business for a fair value of $65. Shares are a bit above this level, but with Mitsui actively buying shares and it and the family ultimately owning about 30% of the company, I see the restricted float likely keeping shares at a premium multiple. Plus, markets will price in some potential that in the long run (beyond 3 years), Mitsui buys the company outright, though this is not my base case.

Giving a 5-10% premium for these technical factors, I can support WRB being worth up to $68-71. This is just under 4% upside from current levels. While WRB remains an extremely well-run and disciplined insurance company, this continues to priced in by markets. As such, I see shares as a “hold,” trading around fair value and I view other insurers like CB as a more attractive alternative, given equally strong management but a more reasonable valuation.
